name: Clara Siagian
affiliation: Bartlett Development Planning Unit, University College London
tagline:
Hi, I'm Clara Siagian. I'm an early career scholar whose work asks how urban development, governance, and public interventions are experienced from the standpoint of those who live with their consequences every day.  

I am currently Postdoctoral Researcher at the Bartlett Development Planning Unit, University College London, and a Research Affiliate with Universitas Indonesia at the Center on Child Protection and Wellbeing (PUSKAPA)
bio:
My research examines how children, youth, and families experience, navigate, resist, and reshape urban interventions—from eviction and social housing to climate adaptation, social protection, education, and digital participation initiatives. 

Across my work, I ask how policies become lived social realities through everyday negotiations, whose experiential knowledge counts in dominant systems of governance, and how these negotiations might be made knowable. Using ethnographic and participatory methods, I treat community members—particularly young people—not simply as research participants but as knowledge producers whose interpretations are central to understanding policy, power, and urban change. 

A central concern across my research is how urban transformation intersects with social reproduction: how families sustain themselves amid displacement, precarious work, and environmental change, and how children and youth emerge not merely as beneficiaries of policy but as actors who participate in making urban futures. 

I completed my PhD at the Crawford School of Public Policy, Australian National University, in November 2024. My doctoral research examined the lived consequences of forced eviction and relocation to state-managed social housing in Jakarta, tracing how displaced residents understand the state and make political claims in the wake of their resettlement. It has been published in Q1 journals such as Cities and IJURR. 

My research has been recognised through competitive awards, including the Australian Government Research Training Program (AGRTP, 2019–2023), the Asian Studies Association of Australia Postdoctoral Writing Grant, and selection as a SEAREG New Faces Fellow at Duke University. I have published in a number of Q1 journal such as Cities, IJURR, and Children’s Geographies, with an forthcoming co-edited book from Oxford University Press. 

Beyond academic works, I also served as consultant on GEDSI and social impact assessment for KONEKSI-DFAT Australia and the World Bank
methods: Long-term ethnographic fieldwork; longitudinal qualitative panels; oral history; participatory mapping with young people; and policy-engaged research with civil society and government partners.
